detail: Chapter 1's forward-map (line ~167) and the Executive Brief (line ~78) both promise a FOUR-part architecture that mis-routes the chapters: they say 'constitutional enforcement (Chapter 4)' and 'a general ledger you verify by replay (Chapter 5)' — but Ch4 is actually Human Primacy and Ch5 is the Constitution; the replay-GL content lives in Ch3. The human gate (Ch4) and the lens (Ch6) — two of the canonical five primitives Ch8 and Reader Resources name — are dropped entirely from this map. The reader-contract is set with the wrong table of contents at the exact sentence that establishes it.
lgp_alignment: A mis-mapped contract erodes the reader's ability to trust the book's own structure — the same generational trust in a provable record the volume sells to heirs and successors.

detail: The $48.2M→$44.9M / $3.3M-deferral correction is run THREE times: Ch1 (opening + Worked Example), Ch2 (Worked Example, as R-1041/R-1198), and the back-matter 'See It Work' (steps 1-5). Ch1 vs Ch2 is defensible (problem-framing vs unit-mechanics), but 'See It Work' is a near-verbatim third pass that advances no argument the reader has not already walked twice — it restates Ch2's R-1041/R-1198 replay rather than demonstrating something new (e.g., a multi-account close, an evidence-tier refusal, or the gate). A back-matter demo that repeats a chapter's move is a structural repetition the reader will feel as filler.
lgp_alignment: Tightening the demo so it advances rather than repeats respects the operator's and the heir's time — minimal-burden stewardship of the reader's attention, an LGP principle.

detail: The Executive Brief (line ~78) lists 'a general ledger you replay instead of trust' as a co-equal fourth pillar alongside receipt/cylinder/constitution, but the volume's canonical count is FIVE primitives (receipt, cylinder, human gate, constitution, lens — per Ch8 line ~861 and Reader Resources line ~994), and 'general ledger by replay' is not one of them — it is a property OF the cylinder (Ch3). The front matter thus implies a different decomposition than the book delivers, leaving the reader to reconcile a four-pillar brief against a five-primitive payoff.
lgp_alignment: A consistent primitive count keeps the foundation legible to a successor who must understand what they inherit — generational continuity depends on a stable vocabulary.

detail: Ch3 introduces 'replay is the source of truth' as its operational heart (line ~383) and Ch6 re-runs the same move as 'visibility that cannot drift from its source' (line ~664). Ch6 does advance it (projections, drift-detection, honest-scope), but the opening of Ch6's first section restates Ch3's replay claim almost in full before adding the new lens material, so the chapter-to-chapter bridge reads as recap rather than build for its first two paragraphs. The Ch5→Ch6 closing bridge (line ~638) sets up 'see them working' well; the Ch6 body should lean on that and not re-derive replay from scratch.
lgp_alignment: Sharpening the Ch3→Ch6 bridge so each chapter adds rather than recaps preserves the reader's forward momentum — minimal burden on the human in the loop, an LGP value.

detail: Back-matter 'Reader Resources' (line ~990) points the reader at three deliverables — 'the series outline,' 'the five primitives, one page,' and 'the verification recipe' — described as if they travel with the book, but none is actually included in the manuscript (no appendix, no one-page summary sheet, no bundled outline). A resources section that names artifacts the buyer cannot find inside the volume is a front/back-matter coherence gap: it promises a kit and ships a pointer.
lgp_alignment: Delivering the verification recipe the book names lets a successor actually replay and prove the chain themselves — hands-on generational verifiability rather than a promise of it.

detail: The 'About This Series' note (line ~33) forward-references Volume 3 (Helix) as already-shown ('Volume 3 showed how a book can write the backend it describes') while this is Volume 1 — a reader who starts at the numbered beginning meets a 'showed' for a volume they have not read. This is a deliberate series-ordering choice (the volumes are written out of sequence and Helix exists), and 'Also by Kenneth Mangum' (line ~1014) re-anchors Helix as Volume 3, so the cross-reference resolves; logged as accepted with the recommendation to soften 'showed' to 'shows' or 'demonstrates' so tense does not imply required prior reading.
lgp_alignment: Keeping each volume independently readable serves families and operators who may inherit one volume without the set — sovereign, standalone continuity of the record.

detail: Ch1 'Mutable by design' asserts SAP S/4HANA's Universal Journal and Oracle's Fusion ledger let 'a posting be changed, reversed, reclassified, or deleted, and when it is, the database does not keep the old version alongside the new one.' A SAP/Oracle practitioner or auditor will flag this as an overclaim: posted FI documents in S/4HANA (ACDOCA) are NOT editable in place for value fields — corrections require reversal/adjustment postings, and document-change rules tightly restrict which non-value fields move. The book's true differentiator (cryptographic seal + replay-from-genesis vs. application-enforced controls) survives a precise statement; the false one (these ledgers silently overwrite rows) does not and undercuts the book's own credibility on rigor.
lgp_alignment: Sovereign credibility with auditors and heirs depends on the book never overclaiming against named incumbents; an honest comparison is what makes the generational trust argument defensible.

detail: Ch3 states the non-negotiable: every economic event 'touches at least two accounts, and the debits equal the credits, always,' and a cylinder 'contains both legs.' Yet the $3.3M revenue deferral — the book's spine example, repeated in Ch1, Ch2 (R-1198), Ch8, and See-It-Work — is described one-sided as 'a $3.3 million deferral' and never names its credit leg. A revenue deferral is Dr Revenue $3.3M / Cr Deferred Revenue (a liability) $3.3M. As written, the flagship correction appears to violate the very double-entry rule Ch3 makes load-bearing. Either show both legs (Dr Revenue / Cr Deferred Revenue) at least once, or state explicitly that 'deferral' is shorthand for the balanced cylinder.
lgp_alignment: Generational stewardship of a ledger requires the worked examples themselves model correct double-entry; an heir replaying a one-sided correction inherits an unbalanced proof.

detail: Ch3 'Replay is the source of truth' and Ch1's $48.2M→$44.9M both assert the original reported figure is reproducible 'exactly as the board saw it.' Rigor caveat: replay reproduces the *posted ledger* state at a sealed point, not necessarily the *reported financial-statement* figure, which can differ from the raw ledger due to consolidation, eliminations, reclasses, and disclosure-only adjustments made outside the GL. The claim 'the number the board saw, reproduced exactly' should be scoped to ledger-derived figures, or note that statement-level reporting overlays must also be sealed to make that guarantee total.
lgp_alignment: Human-primacy reporting to a board demands the book not conflate ledger truth with statement truth; sovereign accuracy means scoping the guarantee precisely for successors.

detail: Ch3 'Evidence travels with the entry' defines E0 as 'rejected, not stored,' but Ch3 'Your Next Steps' step 1 tells the reader to grade five existing entries E0/E1/E2 and says 'the share of E0/E1 is your proof debt.' If a disciplined core cannot store an E0 entry at all, an entry inside the core cannot carry an E0 tier — the two statements are internally inconsistent. Resolve by distinguishing E0 as a pre-write rejection state (cannot seal) from the reader's audit of a *legacy mutable* system where ungraded/claim-only entries do exist and represent proof debt.
lgp_alignment: Sovereign evidence discipline requires the tier ladder be internally consistent end-to-end; an heir auditing the chain must not meet a tier the book says cannot exist.

detail: Ch3 worked example debits Cash $250,000 on customer payment, then a month later books a $5,000 bank fee Dr Bank Fees / Cr Cash. Arithmetic is correct ($245,000 net). But a controller will note the fact pattern is slightly forced: if a $5k fee was deducted by the bank on that settlement, only $245k of cash was actually received, so debiting Cash a full $250k at C-882 implies the fee was a separate later debit (e.g., a monthly account fee), not netted from the receipt. Either frame the fee as an independently-incurred bank charge (cleaner) or, if netted from the settlement, the original receipt should reflect $245k cash + a $5k fee leg.
lgp_alignment: Continuity of trust rests on worked examples that survive a controller's scrutiny; a forced cash fact pattern invites the exact 'this wouldn't really post that way' objection sovereignty must preempt.

detail: Ch1's auditor passage states a mutable-ledger auditor 'cannot replay the year from first principles, because the first principles are gone — the source rows were updated in place,' and 'they sample.' This is fairly stated as a tendency but slightly overstated as universal: auditors sample for cost/scope and risk reasons even where full source data survives, and modern data-analytics audits already test full populations on systems (incl. SAP) that retain source documents via reversal patterns. Soften from 'cannot replay because the rows are gone' to 'cannot independently prove the prior state from the ledger alone, because corrections were not preserved as first-class, verifiable history.'
lgp_alignment: An honest account of audit practice protects the book's sovereign standing with the very auditors and heirs it courts; overstating 'rows are gone' is the kind of claim a regulator-facing heir cannot defend across generations.

detail: Ch4 worked example surfaces a $1.2M vendor payment 'matched to an approved invoice, evidence tier E2.' Rigor note: matching to an approved invoice is a three-way-match control (PO/receipt/invoice), which is an E1 artifact-pointer condition, not automatically E2 (artifact PLUS independent verification hash, per Ch3's own definition). Calling a matched-invoice payment 'E2' without naming the verification step blurs the E1/E2 boundary the book is trying to make crisp. Specify what re-verifiable artifact lifts this to E2, or label it E1.
lgp_alignment: Sovereign control integrity depends on evidence tiers meaning the same thing in every chapter; loose E2 labeling erodes the graded-proof discipline heirs rely on.

detail: Ch5 'What goes in the founding rules' lists 'balances are replayed, never stored as independent facts' as an Integrity rule, while Ch3 introduces periodically-sealed checkpoints ('a sealed trial balance at each period close') that ARE stored, derived balances. A literalist auditor reading the constitution will see 'never stored' and the checkpoint mechanism as in tension. Reconcile by stating the constitutional rule as 'no balance is authoritative unless derivable by replay; checkpoints are sealed, replay-verifiable accelerators, not independent facts that may disagree with the chain.'
lgp_alignment: A constitution an heir replays must be internally coherent with the engine; 'never stored' vs. sealed checkpoints is exactly the gap between claim and proof the sovereign core exists to close.

detail: Ch6 'Audit-ready by default' implies an auditor 'replays the chain themselves... and confirms the views against the receipts in real time,' positioning the immutable core as near-self-auditing. Rigor caveat: independent assurance still requires the auditor to test that the *mapping from real-world events to receipts* is complete and faithful (the recording-completeness assertion) — replay proves internal integrity of what was recorded, not that all economic events were recorded or recorded correctly. The chapter should concede that replay collapses the integrity/existence testing burden but does not eliminate completeness and occurrence testing.
lgp_alignment: Generational assurance is honest only if the book distinguishes provable internal integrity from external completeness; overselling self-audit risks the trust an heir places in the chain.

detail: Ch7's trust-axes table asserts for mutable systems that balances are 'stored, and can disagree with their transactions,' stated as a flat property. In practice well-controlled mutable GLs reconcile sub-ledgers to the GL and balances are derived from posted line items, so 'can disagree' is a risk, not a guaranteed defect. The comparison is more defensible to a board if framed as 'stored balances CAN silently diverge and the system does not structurally prevent it' rather than implying incumbents routinely carry balances that disagree with their detail.
lgp_alignment: A board-facing comparison must be defensible to incumbent-literate directors; sovereign honesty about where mutable cores actually fail is what makes the choice durable across generations of stewards.

detail: Ch8 worked example claims an 18-month outcome where 'the subsidiary's first audit on the new core closed faster and cleaner than any before it, because the auditor replayed rather than sampled,' presented as a concrete result. As an illustrative composite this is fine, but it reads as an empirical case without the 'illustrative' framing the front-matter disclaimer covers generally. A skeptical reader (or an auditor) may take it as a cited outcome. Add an explicit 'illustrative composite' tag to this and the eighteen-months-on narrative so it is not mistaken for a documented engagement result.
lgp_alignment: Sovereign credibility for heirs requires illustrative outcomes be unmistakably labeled as such; an unlabeled success story is the kind of soft claim downstream auditors discount.

detail: The book's core guarantee stops at the cryptographic boundary but is sold as total. A hostile auditor's first question: who holds the signing keys, and what stops the keyholder from re-sealing a forged chain from genesis? An append-only hash chain is only immutable against an attacker WITHOUT the signing key; the owner who controls the key can rebuild a parallel 'true' chain. The book never mentions key custody, key rotation, HSM, threshold signing, or external anchoring (notarization to a third party / public chain). Without that, 'cannot lie about its past' collapses to 'cannot lie unless the owner wants to' — the exact trust model mutable ERP has.
lgp_alignment: Generational continuity for heirs is void if a single keyholder can forge the inheritance record; sovereign custody must be addressed or the guarantee passed to heirs is hollow.

detail: 'Fails closed' (Ch4) and 'cannot seal' (Ch5) are presented as properties of math/construction, but they are properties of CODE the book never exhibits and cannot prove correct. A skeptical CFO knows fail-closed gates fail OPEN constantly in practice (a bypassed check, an exception path, an admin override, a bug). The seal/chain math is genuinely tamper-evident; the GATE enforcing human approval is ordinary software with the ordinary failure modes the book elsewhere mocks ('logging off just for this migration'). The book must concede the gate is the soft spot and is only as strong as its implementation + test coverage, not a mathematical guarantee.
lgp_alignment: Human primacy as a sovereign guarantee for the owner is only real if the gate provably holds; overclaiming it as math erodes the stewardship trust the book asks heirs to rely on.

detail: The checkpoint-vs-genesis-replay tradeoff (Ch3) is presented as 'you do not have to choose' — but a contrarian engineer sees the checkpoint REINTRODUCES the stored-balance trust problem the book spent Ch3 destroying. A checkpoint IS a stored balance. The book says checkpoints are 'verified' and 'sealed,' but a sealed wrong checkpoint replays fast and wrong; you only catch it by genesis-replay, which is the expensive path the checkpoint exists to avoid. So in steady-state production nobody genesis-replays, and the system trusts checkpoints exactly as a mutable core trusts stored balances. The book must address checkpoint-poisoning and when genesis-replay actually gets run.
lgp_alignment: A foundation heirs and auditors are told to 'replay, not trust' must not quietly reintroduce trusted state; sovereign verifiability fails if continuity rests on checkpoints no one re-derives.

detail: Ch7 strawmans the incumbent on two of its five trust axes. (a) 'Can it prove a prior-period figure? Mutable: only via separate audit trail.' SAP's audit trail and document-change logs, when configured per GoBD/SOX, ARE the legally-accepted proof and have survived hostile audits for decades — the book waves this away as 'kept by discipline' without conceding it is also legally sufficient. (b) 'Who owns the ledger? Mutable: the vendor's database.' On-prem SAP HANA runs on the customer's hardware with the customer's DBAs; 'rent from a vendor' conflates SaaS with on-prem and is unfair. A SAP-loyal CIO would shred these two rows. The book needs to fight on the axes it genuinely wins, not inflate the win.
lgp_alignment: A comparison a successor must defend to a board loses its sovereign credibility if it is caught strawmanning; honest stewardship requires conceding the incumbent's real, legally-recognized strengths.

detail: The book's evidentiary spine is partly circular and a scholarly reviewer will name it. Every chapter's RECEIPT box 'proves' the claim by pointing at the book's OWN seal chain ('replay this volume's chain from genesis → it proves itself'). But a seal chain proving itself is not independent verification — it is the system asserting its own integrity by its own rules. The Ch8 receipt literally says 'replay this volume's own chain → it proves itself,' which is the petitio principii: the thing in question (is the chain trustworthy) is assumed by the proof (the chain says so). The book needs at least one EXTERNAL anchor (third-party timestamp, published root hash) or it must drop the claim that the book itself constitutes proof rather than illustration.
lgp_alignment: A generational record that certifies itself by its own internal rules cannot ground human-primacy trust; sovereign proof for heirs requires an anchor outside the system's own assertions.

detail: The migration concession (Ch7/Ch8) is honest about EFFORT but silent on the killer integration problem: an immutable core run 'alongside' SAP for one subsidiary must still consolidate, reconcile, and feed the group's mutable GL, tax engine, and statutory reporting. The boundary between the immutable island and the mutable mainland is where truth degrades — the moment a replayed figure is exported into the incumbent's consolidation, it becomes a copy, mutable again. The book sells 'foundation-first coexistence' without addressing that the coexistence seam re-mutates every number that crosses it, undercutting the provability for exactly the consolidated figures a regulator tests.
lgp_alignment: Continuity of provable truth across the enterprise the heir inherits fails at the integration seam; sovereign coexistence must address where the immutable record re-enters mutable systems.

detail: The heir framing is mostly earned but rests on one unexamined assumption presented as obvious: that an heir WANTS to or CAN replay a cryptographic chain. Ch2 says the successor can 'sit down, open the receipt... and walk its provenance themselves' — but most heirs are not technical, will trust whatever cockpit renders, and thus re-inherit exactly the 'trust the tool' problem one layer up. The generational guarantee quietly depends on the heir running an independent verifier, which the book never asks whether heirs will actually do. It is close to sentimental filler at the points where it asserts the heir's capability rather than the record's.
lgp_alignment: The generational/heir promise is the book's LGP heart; it must be earned by addressing whether non-technical successors can actually exercise the sovereign verification, not assumed.

detail: Every 'Industry Signal' box hedges with '(Directional industry context — confirm against current data before citing specifics.)' A hostile reviewer reads this as the book asserting market tailwinds (restatement counts, full-population testing, continuous auditing as 'the stated direction of the profession') while pre-disclaiming any obligation to be right. Eight unsourced directional claims, each load-bearing for the 'the world is moving our way' thesis, with a blanket liability hedge. Either cite 2-3 of the strongest with a real source, or downgrade the rhetorical weight; as written it is assertion dressed as trend.
lgp_alignment: A sovereign work handed to heirs and boards must not lean its thesis on unsourced trend claims; honest stewardship cites or softens rather than asserting-and-disclaiming.

detail: Ch5's versioned-constitution amendment path has an unaddressed self-reference: if the constitution can be amended by 'a new sealed, human-approved event,' then the rule governing WHO may amend the constitution is itself amendable by that same process — a keyholder with approval authority can vote themselves looser rules going forward (e.g., lower the E1 revenue floor) entirely 'legally' and sealed. The chain proves the rule was changed, but does not prevent capture. The book frames the constitution as a guardrail without conceding it is a guardrail the guarded party can move, given the keys and the approval role (ties to R3-1/R3-2).
lgp_alignment: Constitutional rules meant to protect generational/sovereign continuity must concede the governance-capture risk, or the guarantee to future stewards is overstated.

detail: The book never engages the strongest pure-incumbent counter: a regulator or auditor today does NOT accept 'replay our proprietary chain' as evidence — they accept the formats and attestations they already know (SOC reports, signed PDFs, standard audit trails). A novel cryptographic ledger may be MORE provable in theory yet LESS accepted in practice, creating audit friction the book never costs. 'Audit-ready by default' assumes the auditor is willing and able to replay your chain; many will charge more, not less, to learn a bespoke verification model. The book should concede adoption-of-method risk alongside the migration and performance concessions it already makes.
lgp_alignment: A foundation a successor must defend to real auditors must address that institutional acceptance, not theoretical provability, governs whether the sovereign record actually protects the heir.

detail: Ch3 claims the obligation cylinder carries GL double-entry — 'debit Cash $250,000, credit Accounts Receivable $250,000' as one balanced sealed unit across two accounts. Verified against src/sovereign_agent/obligations/ledger.py (open()=type:'debit'/draft line 223, close()=type:'credit' line ~375) and evidence/actions_projection.py:20 {'debit':'open','approval':'approve','credit':'close'}: the shipped dr/cr is the OBLIGATION lifecycle, not GL-account double-entry. grep for gl_account|double.entry|trial.balance across src/ returns nothing. The Ch3 RECEIPT 'REAL EVENT' points at this ledger as if it already balances two GL legs. Fix: one sentence distinguishing the shipped obligation-ledger primitive (open=debit/close=credit) from the GL double-entry accounting model the book specifies as the target the cylinder is designed to carry.
lgp_alignment: Preserves generational trust and human primacy: an heir who replays the chain must find the primitive matches the book's promise, not a near-miss — honest substrate is the sovereign inheritance this series stakes its name on.

detail: Ch3/Ch6 RECEIPT boxes cite 'scripts/ledger_manifest.py manifest|verify' as the verification mechanism. Ran PYTHONPATH=src python3 scripts/ledger_manifest.py verify → 'Chain OK: True · {open:10,closed:90,total:100}' and manifest → chain_entries:236, chain_valid:true, last_hash:7018117054779d2c. The claim 'state is derived from history, not asserted' holds: verify_chain() recomputes the sha256-16 chain. Minor only because the book says 'replay from genesis → balance computed' while the manifest verifies the HASH chain integrity + obligation counts, not GL-account balances — adjacent but not identical operations a literal reader could conflate.
lgp_alignment: Continuity for heirs: a verification command that runs clean today is the stewardship proof the book sells — the reader can replay the chain and find it holds, which is the generational guarantee.

detail: Ch4 RECEIPT claims a material event 'fails closed' and is 'principal-stamped'. Verified in ledger.py: close() line ~325 raises PermissionError when (material and not rejected and not _is_approved) — the audit-2026-06-13 MED note confirms the guard no longer depends on self.gate being wired, so a gate-less ledger cannot self-approve. open() stamps principal_id (line 228) and material=True (line 232); approve() records approved_by + principal_id (lines 280-283). The Propose→Approve→Execute sequence and 'human disposition is the sealing condition' are faithful to the code. Accepted: the SOURCE (principal stamped) and INTEGRITY (state change gated) triad legs are enforced end-to-end.
lgp_alignment: Human primacy made structural: the owner's hand on every material lever, principal-stamped and non-transferable, is the sovereign control that protects the heirs' inheritance from unauthorized automated action.

detail: All 8 figures are one-line [VISUAL: Figure N.1 ...] stubs (e.g. line 117 Fig 1.1 mutable-vs-cylinder, line 675 Fig 6.1 three-live-projections, line 874 Fig 8.1 five-primitives-plus-governance-stack). None carries a panel-level spec: no label list, no max element count, no print-vs-tile variant. Fig 6.1 must show receipt browser + replayed ledger + pending-gate lens as THREE projections of ONE chain — that is 3 panels + a shared spine in a single figure, which will not read at 6x9 print scale and will not survive shrinking into one Atrium tile. Fig 8.1 (five primitives + a governance stack rising above) is even denser. A designer cannot build the Atrium panel from these stubs.
lgp_alignment: A figure a non-technical heir cannot read at a glance breaks the generational-handoff promise — the successor must SEE the chain proves itself, so figure legibility is a human-primacy/stewardship requirement, not decoration.

detail: The per-chapter AHA exists but is smothered by injected SEO keyword phrases — 'regulated ERP alternative' (Ch1), 'private ERP ledger' (Ch3), 'enterprise ERP framework' (Ch5), 'sovereign ERP vs SAP' (Ch7) — bolded mid-sentence where the HOOK should sit. The genuinely viral lines are already in the text but un-isolated: 'You cannot out-control an architecture whose default is overwrite. You can only change the default.' (line 127) and 'Mutable truth is not generational. It expires the moment the people who vouched for it are gone.' (line 150). Neither is pulled out as a standalone quotable. No chapter ships a <280-char hook ready to paste into an X post or a carousel slide; the keyword bolding actively competes with the hook bolding for the reader's eye.
lgp_alignment: The viral hook is how this sovereign/generational message opts-in new operators (LGP = viral opt-in, not control); burying it under SEO filler suppresses the continuity-of-prosperity reach the book is meant to seed.

detail: 'See It Work' (lines 970-988) is a flawless 5-step NARRATIVE — seal R-1041, seal R-1198, replay to today=$44.9M, replay to close=$48.2M, verify integrity — but it never names a single UI affordance: no screen, no button, no field, no starting state, no 'click here.' It says 'Ask the account its balance now' without saying WHERE the user asks. To become an actual Atrium demo a user could click, each step must bind to a surface (e.g. step 3 → open replayed-ledger view, set as-of=today; step 4 → drag as-of slider to close date; step 5 → integrity-check button turns the altered receipt red). As written it cannot be handed to a UI engineer as a demo script, and it cannot be screen-recorded for a launch video.
lgp_alignment: The replay walkthrough IS the proof a successor/heir performs themselves without trusting anyone — if it never becomes a clickable surface, the generational human primacy 'verify it yourself' promise stays words, not a tool an heir can actually operate.

detail: Reader-facing value maps to UI surfaces strongly in Ch2/4/6 (receipt-as-card line 287, gate-as-home-screen line 497, three windows line 673) but the Atrium mentions are scattered prose asides, not a consolidated 'here are your three screens' anchor. 'Reader Resources' (lines 990-998) lists outline / five-primitives-page / verification-recipe but NOT a single Atrium surface map, so a reader who finishes the book has no one place that says 'value X lives on screen Y.' A one-page surface map (provable books → receipt browser; replay a prior balance → replayed ledger w/ as-of control; human gate → pending-gate inbox) would close the book→cockpit loop and double as a product landing wireframe.
lgp_alignment: A consolidated surface map turns the book's promises into a cockpit an operator and their heirs can navigate — making the sovereign value legible and inheritable rather than dispersed across prose.

detail: Figures 4.1 (Propose→Approve→Execute) and 7.1 (five trust axes side-by-side) are the two MOST UI-ready figures in the book — 4.1 is literally the pending-gate inbox state machine and 7.1 is a comparison table — yet the manuscript does not flag them as the figures that should be built first / reused directly as Atrium components. 7.1's five axes (prove prior figure / silent alteration / balance source / who authors / who owns) are a ready-made carousel: five slides, mutable-vs-immutable per slide. This is a strength left unconverted: the highest-leverage virality+UX asset in the book is not signposted as such.
lgp_alignment: The trust-axis comparison is the clearest sovereign-vs-rented-ledger contrast for a board or heir; surfacing it as the lead carousel spreads the human-primacy/ownership message with minimal burden — direct LGP viral leverage.

detail: Outline conformance gap: every one of the 8 locked chapter cards lists an 'Atrium [surface]' beat (Ch1 Atrium policy surface, Ch2 Atrium Roles surface, Ch3 implied, Ch4 Atrium decision surface, Ch5 Atrium industry lenses, Ch6 Atrium extension flow, Ch7 Atrium federation views, Ch8 Atrium governance cockpit). No body chapter contains an Atrium surface section; Atrium appears only in the 'See It Work' back matter as a single generic walkthrough. Eight declared beats are undelivered in the chapters that promised them — the leader is not conformed to.
lgp_alignment: A governance cockpit a non-technical successor or heir can operate is core to generational continuity; dropping the per-chapter surface beat weakens the human-primacy handoff the LGP close promises.

detail: Chapter 5 structural redundancy: the 'Honest about the library's maturity' section ends with a full clinic walkthrough ('The same engine, the clinic instead of the construction firm... a record_access action class that seals every read, and a disclosure class gated on consent'), performing the job of a worked example. Then the designated 'Worked Example — the retention-hold pattern' runs construction and again back-references the clinic ('The same engine, with a different declared pattern, would run the clinic's patient-data controls'). The clinic/construction contrast is spent twice; the chapter repeats its own move and dilutes the dedicated Worked Example slot.
lgp_alignment: A legible, non-repeating vertical chapter is what lets a sovereign operator and their successors actually read and reuse a pattern; redundancy taxes the human-ease the families-first surface aims for.

detail: Reader-contract over-claim in Chapter 5: the Promise reads 'You will apply the governance skin to real vertical needs,' but the body's 'Honest about the library's maturity' section concedes 'What does not yet exist is a deep, pre-built library of industry patterns... they do not get a turnkey catalog for their exact industry on day one.' The Promise verb 'apply' implies ready-made application; the honest delivery is 'you can author your own patterns; none ship.' The promise is not paid off as stated — soften the Promise to 'shape/declare' or have the chapter open by reframing 'apply' as 'author and declare,' so the contract matches the honest body.
lgp_alignment: Honest promise-to-payoff matching is the stewardship discipline the whole RECEIPT mechanism embodies; an over-claimed promise undercuts the sovereign-trust the volume sells across generations.

detail: Forward-pointer asymmetry across volumes: Chapter 6 names its forward volume precisely ('Volume 3 (Helix) already demonstrates a book that extrudes its own backend'), but Chapter 4 ('the subject of later volumes'), Chapter 7 ('the federation work of the broader series'), and Chapter 8 ('the later volumes build the automation, the value flows, and the federation') point forward only vaguely. The federation forward-map in Ch7's RECEIPT ('the broader series' federation volumes') never names a volume the way Helix is named, so the reader cannot tell whether federation is a numbered volume or a separate line.
lgp_alignment: A precise series map is what lets a successor or heir navigate the generational body of work; vague forward-routing erodes the continuity the sovereign series promises.

detail: Back-matter 'Also by' asymmetry: the body (Ch4/Ch6/Ch7) and the 'Connect' note both promise 'later volumes build the automation, value flows, and federation,' yet 'Also by Kenneth Mangum' lists only Volume 1, Volume 3 (Helix), and two companion lines — no automation/value-flow/federation volume placeholder. A reader told repeatedly that those volumes exist 'in the series' finds Helix listed but the others absent, leaving the back matter incoherent with the in-body forward-pointers.
lgp_alignment: Coherent back matter is how the reader follows the sovereign body of work forward to their heirs; a gap between promised and listed volumes weakens the generational map.

detail: Near-repeat of the 'clean revocation, the record shows the exact instant' move across Ch7 and Ch8. Ch7's 'Boundaries you can prove' section: 'the mandate is withdrawn as a single sealed event, and the chain shows the exact instant the boundary closed.' Ch8 reuses the same beat for succession: 'replay the history and see how each rule came to be... Nothing was lost at the handoff.' Both turn on clean endings provable by replay. Distinct enough in register (partner offboarding vs generational handoff) to keep, but the phrasing and rhetorical move are close; tighten one so Ch8's close feels earned rather than echoed.
lgp_alignment: Ch8 is the LGP close and must land with its own generational force; leaning on Ch7's revocation move risks the continuity finale reading as a reprise rather than a culmination.

detail: Bridge precision at Ch3→Ch4 vs the gate's first appearance: Chapter 3 already stages the gate substantively (the journal_adjustment worked example gates the $400k adjustment, and the 'Scope, condition, gate, evidence' list defines gate before Ch4). Ch3's bridge says 'Chapter 4 makes the breath-gate its own primitive.' By the time the reader reaches Ch4, the gate has been demonstrated end-to-end once already, so Ch4's 'The gate, declared and generalized' must work to feel like new ground rather than a formalization of what Ch3 already showed. Verify Ch4 earns its own chapter rather than re-running Ch3's gate demonstration.
lgp_alignment: A breath-gate chapter that adds genuine new ground (fail-closed guarantee, automation-binding, governance-record-for-free) rather than repeating reinforces the human-in-the-loop primacy the sovereign governance model rests on.

detail: Executive Brief / Preflight overlap of the 'bolded lines are the argument' device. The epigraph-adjacent 'About This Series' says 'Read the bolded lines and you have the argument; read the rest and you have the proof.' Preflight 'How to read it' repeats verbatim: 'The argument is in the bolded lines — read only those and you have the case. Read the rest and you have the proof.' The same instruction is delivered twice within two pages of front matter; consolidate to one statement so the front matter does not stutter before Chapter 1.
lgp_alignment: Tight front matter respects the operator's and successor's time — the minimal-burden, human-primacy principle that lets the sovereign governance skin be inherited and run with generational continuity, not friction.

detail: Ch3 (lines 355-363) defines SOD correctly in prose ('the person who creates a transaction must not be the one who approves it, and the approver must not be the one who reconciles') but then implements it as only 'the approve step of a payment requires a principal different from the one who invoked it.' That invoker!=approver rule is dual-control / four-eyes — a TWO-party check — not full segregation of duties. SOD is a multi-function separation (initiate / authorize / record / reconcile / custody) plus a role-conflict matrix preventing one principal from holding incompatible roles across transactions; an invoker!=approver predicate does not cover the approver-vs-reconciler leg the same sentence names, nor toxic role combinations, nor collusion. As written, a reader designing the control would under-build it and an auditor would flag the gap.
lgp_alignment: Correct SOD framing protects human primacy in the control: a successor or auditor inheriting this book must not mistake four-eyes for full duty separation, or generational continuity rests on a weaker control than claimed.

detail: The $50,000 payment-gate worked example is internally inconsistent across the book. Ch1 (lines 156-158) declares 'payments over $50,000 require a second approver from Finance' with gate 'require: [finance_approver, second_approver]' — i.e. TWO approvers (a dual-approval / multi-approver control). Ch4's worked example (lines 478-484) and the 'See It Work' demo (lines 938, 942-944) describe the same $50,000 gate as requiring ONE human disposition ('until a human accepts'). A controls book cannot specify the same gate as both two-approver and single-approver; this is a coherence defect in the load-bearing example. Pick one model (single breath-gate vs. dual-approval) and make Ch1, Ch4, and See It Work agree, or distinguish them explicitly as two different gate types.
lgp_alignment: A self-consistent worked example is the book holding itself to the honesty it asks of the ERP; an inconsistent gate spec undermines the sovereign reader's ability to prove the rule they run by — directly against the LGP claim of legible, provable governance.

detail: Ch2 (lines 224-234) calls the role model a 'lattice' that is 'least-privilege by construction.' In access-control theory a lattice (Denning / Bell-LaPadula) is a partial order with defined least-upper-bound and greatest-lower-bound operators; what the chapter describes — roles composed from capabilities with declared supersede/delegate relations — is an RBAC role hierarchy, which is a partial order but not necessarily a lattice (no guaranteed join/meet). 'Least-privilege BY CONSTRUCTION' also overclaims: least privilege is a property of how grants are AUTHORED, not something the structure guarantees — you can over-provision in any RBAC model. Recommend 'least-privilege by default / by discipline' and either justify the lattice algebra or soften 'lattice' to 'role hierarchy / composition graph.'
lgp_alignment: Precise IAM language keeps the governance skin credible to the architects who must inherit and extend it; sovereign tooling that misuses control-theory terms loses the trust that makes it inheritable across generations.

detail: Ch7 (lines 761-767) claims offboarding leaves 'no lingering orphaned permission, because the authority was never a scatter of grants — it was one scoped mandate, granted as an event and revoked as one.' True only WITHIN the governed engine and only IF the party never accumulated side-grants. A CISO would flag that real offboarding risk also lives outside the engine — already-exported data, live sessions/tokens, cached credentials, downstream replicated systems — none of which a single sealed revocation reaches. The absolute 'no lingering orphaned permission' overstates. Recommend caveating to 'no lingering grant inside the governed system' and acknowledging out-of-engine residue (exfiltrated data, external copies) as out of scope of the seal.
lgp_alignment: Honest boundary-claims protect the human steward from false assurance at offboarding — the moment continuity is most fragile; overclaiming revocation completeness is exactly the trust-evaporation the LGP close warns against.

detail: Three of the eight Industry Signal bold lines assert empirical RANKINGS as fact, and their caveats only license confirming 'specifics' — not the ranking itself. Ch3 (line 380): 'where material weaknesses are most often found'; Ch7 (line 799): 'a top-ranked source of breach and audit exposure'; Ch5 (line 592): 'a leading source of cost, risk, and upgrade paralysis.' 'Most often,' 'top-ranked,' and 'leading' are quantitative claims about relative frequency/rank stated assertively in the bold (argument-carrying) line, while the parenthetical only asks the reader to confirm 'specifics before citing.' A rigorous reader treats a ranking as a hard claim needing a source. Recommend softening the bold lines to attributed/hedged form ('a frequently cited source,' 'commonly flagged among the leading risks') so the assertion does not outrun the caveat.
lgp_alignment: Caveats that fully cover their own claims keep the book's honesty key intact; an empirical ranking asserted past its hedge erodes the legible-and-provable standard the sovereign series stakes its credibility on.

detail: Ch2 (lines 247-255) claims legible YAML grants make access recertification 'tractable rather than dreaded' by turning it into 'a review of data a manager can actually understand.' Legibility helps, but an IAM practitioner would note recertification burden is driven as much by SCALE (thousands of grants), by SOD/toxic-combination detection across grants, and by attestation workflow as by the readability of any single grant — none of which legible YAML alone solves. The claim as stated implies legibility largely dissolves the recert problem. Recommend narrowing to 'makes each grant reviewable' while acknowledging scale, conflict-detection, and attestation tooling remain real work — consistent with the book's own 'honest about scope' discipline elsewhere.
lgp_alignment: Right-sizing the recertification claim preserves the operator's realistic expectation of stewardship effort; overpromising ease at the review step undercuts the durable, least-privilege-over-time governance the volume promises across generations.

detail: EXTERNAL-ANCHOR / KEY-CUSTODY OVERCLAIM, cross-volume. Ch8 CLAIM says governance is 'independently verifiable against an external anchor'; the Ch8 VERIFY line says 'anchor-checked, they are provable to outsiders'; 'See It Work' step 5 says 'the current root checkable against the external anchor.' None of these is marked DESIGNED-TOWARD. But the anchor is exactly the discipline Volume 1 itself frames as something 'the constitution should require from day one' (split-key custody held in hardware + rotation, root published to an independent witness) — i.e. a required design posture, NOT demonstrated as live. Volume 1's own Ch8 RECEIPT bundles 'check its root against an external anchor' into the VERIFY procedure with no DESIGNED-TOWARD caveat either, so V2 inherits and amplifies V1's ambiguity rather than correcting it. A hostile auditor's kill-shot: without a published anchor + split key, V1 concedes immutability is only 'immutability against anyone without the key' — so 'provable to outsiders' collapses to 'provable to anyone who trusts the keyholder,' the very thing the series says it defeats. The book must add a DESIGNED-TOWARD line to the Ch8 RECEIPT naming anchor-publication and split-key custody as design-direction, and soften 'See It Work' step 5 ('checkable' -> 'checkable once an anchor is published').
lgp_alignment: Independent verifiability against an anchor no single keyholder controls is THE mechanism by which an heir or regulator trusts the books without trusting the founder — overclaiming it as live undermines the generational/sovereign continuity the LGP thesis rests on.

detail: YAML-LEGIBILITY OVERSOLD; the strongest counter is raised and left unanswered. Ch1 concedes the risk in one sentence ('let it sprawl and it becomes the very opaqueness it was meant to replace') and 'answers' it with an exhortation ('keep the file small enough to read in a sitting'). That is willpower, not a mechanism — exactly the discipline-not-property failure the book mocks binders for. The SAP-GRC architect's lived counter: real enterprise rule sets are NOT small. Segregation-of-duties matrices alone run to thousands of conflict rules; entity x role x action-class x condition combinatorics explode; OPA/Rego and similar policy-as-code estates are notorious for becoming write-only thickets that only their authors can read. The book asserts legibility-at-scale as a property while every honest practitioner has watched it fail to hold past the toy example. Either the book must concede that legibility-at-scale is itself DESIGNED-TOWARD (requires tooling not shown — linting, conflict-detection, layering, generated human views), or it must show the mechanism that keeps a 3,000-rule estate legible. As written it commits the very sin (claiming a property that is actually an unenforced discipline) Ch1 indicts.
lgp_alignment: Legibility is the load-bearing promise for human primacy and successor stewardship — if it silently fails at scale, the successor inherits opacity wearing a constitution's costume, defeating generational governance continuity.

detail: SOFTWARE-ONLY CONCESSION NOT CARRIED CONSISTENTLY. Ch4 makes the honest concession precisely: the gate is 'enforced in software, not guaranteed by mathematics ... exactly as strong as the code and the tests that hold it shut.' Good. But that same software-only strength governs EVERY other enforcement claim in the book, and elsewhere the prose uses absolute, math-grade language that implies more. Ch2: 'no authority that appeared without a trace.' Ch3: SoD 'enforced on the verb, every time' and 'cannot post a payment that violates the class's conditions.' Ch6: 'it cannot grow its way out of its own governance.' Ch7: the system can prove 'that no one saw or touched what they should not have.' Each of these is, like the gate, software-enforced and therefore only as strong as code+tests — a scope check, an SoD predicate, an extension gate, and a read-boundary are all just code that can have a bug or a bypass, with none of the cryptographic backing the seal chain has. By conceding the caveat only for the gate and stating the others in 'cannot'/'prove' language, the book lets the reader generalize the seal's mathematical strength to enforcement that does not have it. Add one cross-cutting sentence (Ch1 or Ch3) that the same 'software-enforced, as strong as code+tests' caveat applies to ALL skin-level enforcement, and dial back the absolute 'cannot'/'prove no one touched' phrasings to 'enforced and tested to prevent.'
lgp_alignment: Honest naming of where a guarantee is math vs code is the book's core integrity commitment; selectively conceding it for one control and implying invulnerability for the rest erodes the truth-first discipline LGP and human-primacy governance depend on.

detail: 'ONE ENGINE, MANY SHAPES' UNDERSTATES THE FORK PRESSURE FROM CONDITION-EXPRESSIVENESS. Ch5 honestly concedes the *library* of patterns is DESIGNED-TOWARD, but it does NOT address the deeper architectural counter: verticals differ not only in WHICH primitives they compose but in the EXPRESSIVENESS the conditions require. A lien-waiver/retention release, HIPAA minimum-necessary disclosure logic, and an investor waterfall with side-letter carve-outs are not the same 'scope+condition+gate' shape with different values — they demand genuinely different predicate vocabularies, external data joins, and temporal/state logic. The honest risk is that the declarative condition DSL either (a) stays simple and forces real logic back into engine code (a fork by another name) or (b) grows expressive enough to be a programming language — at which point 'legible YAML a non-programmer reads' (the Ch1 thesis) is gone. The book should name this expressiveness/legibility tension as the real DESIGNED-TOWARD risk for 'one engine,' not just the missing catalog.
lgp_alignment: If the engine must fork per vertical to handle real condition logic, the 'verify once, reuse everywhere' trust economy that makes sovereign governance affordable for non-enterprise heirs and operators does not materialize.

detail: 'GOVERNANCE RECORD FOR FREE' AND 'OFFBOARDING AS CLEAN AS ONBOARDING' ARE TOO FRICTIONLESS. Ch4 claims the gate yields 'a complete account of which material decisions were made' as a byproduct; Ch7 claims a withdrawn mandate means 'no lingering orphaned permission.' A hostile auditor pushes back on completeness: the record is complete only for actions that flow THROUGH the governed engine — anything done out-of-band (a DBA touching storage, an integration writing directly, a cached credential, a read that was logged-but-action-taken-elsewhere) is invisible, and the book's own Ch6 'side door' chapter concedes side doors are the historical failure mode. So 'complete account' should be scoped to 'complete account of governed actions,' and the offboarding claim should concede that cleanliness holds only if every grant was issued through the mandate and never duplicated outside it — otherwise the book promises a completeness it cannot structurally guarantee against the very out-of-band paths it elsewhere admits exist.
lgp_alignment: Overstating completeness of the governance record sets a successor or regulator up to over-trust the chain and under-audit the out-of-band surface — a false sense of provability that harms the long-term stewardship LGP seeks.

detail: GENERATIONAL FRAME IS MOSTLY EARNED BUT LEANS SENTIMENTAL AT TWO SEAMS. Ch8 earns most of its inheritance claim mechanically (replayable rules + attributed change history = a readable rulebook a successor inherits) — that part is legitimate and is the book's best generational argument. But two moves slip from mechanism to sentiment: (a) 'the alignment that made the system trustworthy survives the people who built it' — 'alignment' is doing undefined emotional work here; the mechanism only preserves the RULES and their HISTORY, not the JUDGMENT that made them wise, and a successor can faithfully replay a rulebook while completely misunderstanding why a threshold was set — the book should not let 'alignment' imply transmitted wisdom. (b) The Worked Example asserts the successor reads history and sees 'the threshold raised after an incident, the role scoped after a near-miss' — but that rationale is only inheritable if the system actually CAPTURES rationale-at-change, which the book asserts ('seal it with the rationale,' Ch8 step 2) but never shows is enforced rather than optional. If rationale capture is optional, the inherited history is dates and diffs without the 'why,' and the generational promise quietly thins.
lgp_alignment: The generational/heirs claim is the spine of the LGP thesis; conflating 'inherited rules' with 'inherited wisdom/alignment' risks promising stewardship continuity the mechanism does not actually deliver.

detail: INDUSTRY-SIGNAL HEDGING IS HONEST BUT STRUCTURALLY UNFALSIFIABLE — a scholarly reviewer flags the rhetorical pattern. Every chapter's Industry Signal asserts that 'the direction the [audits/breaches/findings] point toward is exactly [this book's design]' and then hedges with '(Directional industry context — confirm before citing specifics.)'. The hedge protects against citation error, but the recurring move — naming a real industry pain, then declaring the book's architecture the direction that pain 'points toward' — is unfalsifiable: any pain can be narrated as pointing toward one's own solution. A contrarian auditor reads seven of these and discounts all of them as motivated reasoning. The book would be stronger conceding, once, that policy-as-code / GRC-automation also has documented FAILURE modes (rule sprawl, alert fatigue, false-confidence in automated controls) — addressing the counter-evidence rather than only the confirming direction would convert advocacy into analysis.
lgp_alignment: Truth-first, evidence-respecting argument is a stewardship obligation; one-directional industry framing that ignores the failure literature weakens the credibility a generational governance thesis needs to earn an heir's and a regulator's trust.

detail: Ch1/Ch3 worked examples present a numeric gate ('payments over $50,000 require a second approver', condition 'amount > 50000', materiality threshold) as the mechanism. Checked src/sovereign_agent/compliance/compliance_engine.py:_charter_v7_ack_required and human_approval_gate.py:_is_high_materiality — gating is by high_materiality_classes membership + keyword match ('board','filing','external','material'), NOT by parsing a numeric '>' predicate from the action-class YAML. role_spec.yaml action_classes carry id/scope/breath_gate/description only; breath_gate is prose ('RED for commitment > threshold'). The book is honest: every RECEIPT marks 'full declarative role/action YAML engine' and 'per-verb scope/condition DSL' as DESIGNED-TOWARD, and the copyright page + Preflight promise to mark runs-today vs design. The dollar-threshold examples read as illustrative, consistent with the receipts. Accepted, not material — no RECEIPT claims the predicate engine RUNS-TODAY.
lgp_alignment: Honest runs-today/designed-toward split protects the generational trust the series sells: a successor inheriting the rulebook must not be misled about which guarantees are math, which are tested code, and which are roadmap — human-primacy depends on that legibility.

detail: Ch4 RECEIPT asserts the human approval gate is 'live and enforced on the core — unapproved material actions are refused (Propose->Approve->Execute), principal-stamped; verified against ledger.py.' Verified directly: ledger.py close() lines 320-329 raise PermissionError when ob.material and not approved and not rejected, with an explicit FAIL-CLOSED comment that the guard no longer depends on self.gate being injected (a gate-less ledger means 'cannot self-approve', not 'no gate'). approve() at line 263 records a principal-stamped 'approval' entry; close() stamps closed_by/principal_id. tests/test_obligation_ledger_governed.py passes (8 tests). The honest caveat (software-enforced, not math; security-critical; adversarially tested) is stated in prose AND the RECEIPT. This is the model honesty case — logged as accepted to record that the strongest claim was checked against code, not taken on faith.
lgp_alignment: A fail-closed, principal-stamped human gate is the structural guarantee of human primacy over automation as the system scales — the LGP-load-bearing control that keeps an aligned system from granting itself authority unsupervised across generations.

detail: Ch6 RECEIPT RUNS-TODAY claims 'Volume 3 (Helix) demonstrates governed self-extrusion under these guardrails.' This is a cross-volume claim, not an in-repo claim, so I treated it as forward-reference and checked only that the repo carries Helix/extrusion machinery consistent with it: hopper.py route describes book->code extrusion of YAML/spec; tests/test_extrusion_anchors.py exists and the broader suite passes; book_code_map.yaml present. The fully-autonomous self-building ERP is explicitly marked DESIGNED-TOWARD ('not unsupervised self-rewrite'). No overclaim found; the runs-today portion (gated, sealed, attributed proposal discipline) maps to the same ledger primitives verified in TA-2. Accepted.
lgp_alignment: Keeping system self-extension inside the same gate as a payment is what lets the ERP grow across generations without growing out of its own governance — sustained alignment is the LGP-positive outcome of 'no growth outside the gate.'

detail: Figure 3.1 (An Action Class) renders as four near-identical dark navy bars — SCOPE/CONDITIONS/GATE/EVIDENCE — differentiated only by small gold sub-caption text (which roles/amounts, auto-or-human, E0/E1/E2). At book-print scale and especially as an Atrium tile thumbnail, the four bars read as one repeated block and the differentiating sub-text is the first thing to vanish, so the tile teaches 'a stack of four things' rather than the load-bearing concept 'one verb carries scope+condition+gate+evidence.' This is the chapter doing the most conceptual work (the action class IS the unit of governance) yet its figure is the least legible at reduction. Differentiate the four rows by icon/shade/left-rail color and promote one concrete value into each row at title weight so it survives a 240px tile.
lgp_alignment: A figure that fails at tile scale breaks the book→cockpit handoff that lets a non-engineer operator actually govern — eroding the human-primacy ease the sovereign ERP promises across generations.

detail: The book's headline reader value is 'read, EDIT, and prove a rule' (subtitle, Exec Brief, Ch8 close), and Chapters 1-2 insist a non-programmer can edit governance. But the only edit surface anywhere in the book is 'See It Work' step 4: 'change the threshold in the YAML to $25,000.' That implies a raw text editor — exactly the programmer-facing surface the book argues against — and no Atrium edit surface (guided form, threshold slider, diff-preview-before-seal) is named. The middle verb of read/edit/prove has no human-first UI translation, which is the weakest seam against the human-ease-first north star. Name a concrete edit surface: a structured rule editor that shows the YAML diff and the to-be-sealed change receipt before commit, so 'edit' becomes a click, not a text file.
lgp_alignment: If editing governance still requires hand-editing YAML, sovereignty stays with engineers, not the operator/heir — defeating the generational aim that the rules be legible AND editable by the human who inherits them.

detail: Figure 2.1 (The Role Lattice): three capability boxes (read ledger / propose pay / approve ≤$50k) converge via crossing gold lines onto 'Divisional Controller,' and the lines collide at the 'composed into' arrowhead into a small visual pile-up. The composition idea is right and reads fine at full page, but at tile/thumbnail scale the crossing diagonals + merged arrowhead muddy into a star-burst rather than 'three capabilities compose into one scoped role.' Straighten to a clean 3→1 funnel (parallel drops into a merge bar) so the compose relationship survives reduction as cleanly as the 4.1/6.1 flow figures do.
lgp_alignment: Least-privilege role composition is the chapter's sovereign-access teaching; if the tile garbles it, the operator can't self-verify who-can-do-what, weakening provable human-controlled authority.

detail: The hooks are excellent but unassembled. Every figure caption is a ready-made hook ('Rules you can read and edit, enforced on a core that cannot lie'; 'the gate makes automation safe, not slow'; 'Verticals are declared patterns on one shared engine — not forks'; 'The business changes hands; the alignment does not change with it'). That is eight carousel slides / one X thread spine sitting in the book — but nothing in Reader Resources packages them as a shareable sequence, so the virality asset exists latent and un-extracted. Add a one-page 'governance skin in 8 lines' card to Reader Resources that lists the eight captions in order — it doubles as the LinkedIn carousel and the thread skeleton.
lgp_alignment: Viral opt-in (not control) is the LGP growth path; un-packaged hooks mean the book's best invitation-to-the-loop never ships, slowing the families-first spread the series depends on.

detail: 'See It Work' step 4 bundles two distinct UI actions into one walkthrough beat: 'Accept the payment (principal-stamped seal), THEN change the threshold in the YAML.' In a real Atrium click-flow these are two different surfaces (the pending-gate disposition vs. the governance-edit surface) and two different sealed events. Bundling them in one demo step blurs the clickable flow and hides that the rule-edit is itself a gated, receipted event — the chapter's whole point. Split into 4a Dispose (disposition surface → approval receipt) and 4b Edit-the-rule (edit surface → rule-change receipt) so the demo flow maps 1:1 to surfaces.
lgp_alignment: Each sealed event being its own visible step proves governance changes are themselves governed — the continuity property a steward/heir must see to trust the generational inheritance.

detail: The demo never exercises the REJECT path. Ch4's worked example explicitly promises both outcomes — 'Accept, and the approval seals... reject, and the chain shows a payment considered and declined' — and the pending-gate inbox ('Awaiting You') is the most demo-able, most viral surface in the cockpit. Yet 'See It Work' only walks the accept path on the $60k payment. A demo (and a screen-recording clip for social) that shows a rejection producing a receipt is the stronger fail-closed proof and the more shareable moment. Add a reject beat: reject one proposal, show the declined-receipt in the history replay.
lgp_alignment: Showing a rejection that still seals a receipt is the clearest demonstration that the human gate holds and is recorded — the human primacy and sovereign control the whole series stands on.

detail: 'See It Work' references surfaces with slightly drifting names — 'the governance view,' 'the action submission; the receipt browser,' 'the pending-gate inbox' (aka 'Awaiting You'), 'the disposition action,' 'the governance-history replay + the anchor check.' For a clickable demo these need to be a fixed, enumerated surface set so the build targets stable panels and the book→UI vocabulary doesn't fork. Lock a named-surface table (Governance View · Action Submit · Receipt Browser · Awaiting-You Inbox · Disposition · History Replay · Anchor Check) in Reader Resources, mirrored to the actual Atrium panel names.
lgp_alignment: A stable shared vocabulary between book and cockpit lets a sovereign operator move from reading to doing without a translator — sustaining legible, self-serviceable governance continuity across generations.
